brief: KNOWLEDGE GAP / WHY IT MATTERS: S-ICD is an alternative to transvenous ICD; avoids intravascular complications in patients without a pacing need. Outcomes of S-ICD implantation in patients with prior sternotomy are uncertain/unknown. Sternotomy alters anterior chest/sternal anatomy potentially affecting lead positioning, sensing, and defibrillation. STUDY DESIGN + SETTING: Multicenter retrospective cohort study. Adult patients with S-ICD implanted between Jan 2014 and June 2020. AIM: compare implant techniques and outcomes of S-ICD between patients with vs without prior sternotomy; also relevant to patients with future sternotomy (per title). POPULATION / SAMPLE / N: Total n=212 patients. Mean age 49 +/- 15 years; 43% women; BMI 30 +/- 8 kg/m2; 68% primary prevention; 30% ischemic cardiomyopathy; median LVEF 30% (IQR 25%-45%). Prior sternotomy in 47 patients (22%); no prior sternotomy in 165 patients. INTERVENTION / COMPARISON: S-ICD implantation; comparison = prior sternotomy group vs no prior sternotomy group. KEY RESULTS WITH NUMBERS: (a) Sensing vector primary: 57% (sternotomy) vs 53% (no sternotomy), p=0.55. (b) Lead laterality leftward to sternum: 94% vs 96%, p=0.54. (c) Defibrillation threshold: 65 +/- 1.4 J vs 65 +/- 0.8 J, p=0.76. (d) 30-day complications: 3/47 (6%) vs 15/165 (9%), p=0.56. (e) Median follow-up 28 months (IQR 10-49 months). (f) Inappropriate shocks: 3/47 (6%) vs 16/165 (10%), p=0.58. MAIN TAKEAWAY: S-ICD implantation in patients with prior sternotomy is safe; similar implant technique parameters (sensing vector, lead laterality, defibrillation threshold), similar 30-day complication rate, and similar inappropriate shock rate compared with patients without prior sternotomy.
